steps.title: Identify what the question asks for
steps.content:
The question asks which equation displays its minimum value as a constant or coefficient—meaning the minimum value must appear directly as one of the stated numbers in the given form.

For example, vertex form $y = a(x - h)^2 + k$ displays the minimum $k$.

We need to check whether the minimum of function I or II equals any of its stated coefficients.

steps.title: Analyze function I on the domain $x > 0$
steps.content:
For $f(x) = 18(1.4)^{x+5}$:
  • The base is $1.4 > 1$, so this is an exponential growth function
  • As $x$ increases from just above 0, the exponent $x + 5$ increases, making $(1.4)^{x+5}$ larger
  • Therefore $f(x)$ is strictly increasing on $(0, \infty)$ with no actual minimum
  • The infimum (greatest lower bound) is $\lim_{x \to 0^+} 18(1.4)^{x+5} = 18(1.4)^5 \approx 96.8$
The displayed coefficients are 18, 1.4, and 5. The infimum 96.8 is not one of these.

steps.title: Analyze function II on the domain $x > 0$
steps.content:
For $g(x) = 18(1.16)(1.4)^{x+3}$:
  • The base is again $1.4 > 1$, so this is also exponential growth
  • For $x > 0$, the exponent $x + 3$ increases, so the function increases
  • This function is also strictly increasing on $(0, \infty)$ with no actual minimum
  • The infimum is $\lim_{x \to 0^+} 18(1.16)(1.4)^{x+3} = 18(1.16)(1.4)^3 \approx 57.3$
The displayed coefficients are 18, 1.16, 1.4, and 3. The infimum 57.3 is not one of these.

steps.title: Conclusion: neither equation displays its minimum as a coefficient
steps.content: Both functions are increasing exponentials whose infimums are never actually attained on the domain $x > 0$. Even if we consider the infimum, it is not a displayed constant or coefficient in either equation. That matches choice D — D is correct.
